learning_objectives: Define management and explain its importance in organizations
Identify the four functions of management
readings: Mintzberg, The Nature of Managerial Work (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Principles of Management. This course provides a comprehensive foundation in how organizations are structured, led, and managed to achieve their goals.
key_concepts: Top, middle, and first-line management roles
The changing nature of management in the 21st century
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case analysis of management structures at Apple, Toyota, and a local small business
setup_requirements: Harvard Business Review access via university library

review.value: Planning (setting goals), Organizing (arranging resources), Leading (motivating people), Controlling (monitoring performance)
Mintzberg's 10 roles across interpersonal, informational, and decisional categories

learning_objectives: Trace the evolution of management theory from classical to modern
Compare scientific management with administrative theory
Evaluate the contributions of the Hawthorne studies
readings: Frederick Taylor, Principles of Scientific Management (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding where management theory came from helps us appreciate why organizations operate the way they do today.
key_concepts: Hawthorne studies and the human relations movement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Timeline exercise mapping management theories to real-world industrial changes

review.value: Taylor's focus on efficiency through time-and-motion studies and systematic task analysis
Workers' productivity improved when they felt observed and valued, highlighting social factors in productivity

learning_objectives: Differentiate between strategic, tactical, and operational planning
Apply SWOT analysis to a real business scenario
Understand rational and bounded rationality decision models

lecture_notes.introduction: Planning is the foundation of all management functions. Without a clear plan, organizations cannot allocate resources effectively or measure progress.
lecture_notes.demonstration: Group SWOT analysis of a publicly traded company using recent annual reports

review.value: Long-term direction setting involving mission definition, environmental analysis, and resource allocation
Herbert Simon's concept that decision makers satisfice rather than optimize due to cognitive limitations

learning_objectives: Identify key elements of organizational structure
Compare mechanistic and organic organizational designs
Evaluate when different structures are most effective
readings: Burns & Stalker, Mechanistic vs. Organic Organizations (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: How an organization is structured directly affects its ability to execute strategy, communicate, and adapt to change.
key_concepts: Chain of command and span of control
lecture_notes.demonstration: Org chart analysis comparing Google, the US military, and Valve Corporation

review.value: High specialization, rigid departmentalization, clear chain of command, narrow spans of control
Cross-functional teams, free information flow, wide spans of control, low formalization

learning_objectives: Compare trait, behavioral, and contingency leadership theories
Apply Maslow's hierarchy and Herzberg's two-factor theory
Distinguish between transactional and transformational leadership

lecture_notes.introduction: Leadership and motivation are at the heart of the 'leading' function. Effective managers must understand what drives people and how to inspire high performance.
key_concepts: McGregor's Theory X and Theory Y
lecture_notes.demonstration: Leadership style self-assessment survey and group discussion of results

review.value: Inspires followers through vision, intellectual stimulation, individualized consideration, and idealized influence
Hygiene factors (salary, conditions) prevent dissatisfaction; motivators (achievement, recognition) drive satisfaction

learning_objectives: Describe the three steps in the control process
Evaluate different types of organizational controls

lecture_notes.introduction: Controlling is the management function that ensures organizational activities conform to plan. Without effective controls, even the best strategies fail.
key_concepts: Balanced scorecard: financial, customer, internal, learning perspectives
Corporate governance and ethics in control
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a balanced scorecard for a hypothetical startup company

review.value: Measure actual performance, compare against standards, take corrective action if deviations exist
Measures performance across financial, customer, internal process, and learning/growth perspectives

questions.question: Explain the difference between efficiency and effectiveness in management, and provide one example of each from a real company.
questions.answer: Efficiency means doing things right (minimizing resource waste), while effectiveness means doing the right things (achieving goals). Example: Toyota's lean manufacturing is efficient (minimal waste), while Apple's focus on user experience is effective (achieving customer delight and market leadership).

rubric.key: Clear distinction between the two concepts

questions.question: A mid-size retail company is losing market share to online competitors. Using the SWOT framework, identify one element for each category (Strength, Weakness, Opportunity, Threat) that such a company might face.
questions.answer: Strength: Established brand recognition and physical store presence allowing customers to touch and try products. Weakness: Higher overhead costs compared to online-only retailers. Opportunity: Develop omnichannel strategy integrating online ordering with in-store pickup. Threat: Continued growth of e-commerce giants like Amazon offering lower prices and faster delivery.

questions.question: Compare and contrast mechanistic and organic organizational structures. Under what environmental conditions is each most effective?
questions.answer: Mechanistic structures feature high specialization, rigid hierarchy, and formal rules; they work best in stable, predictable environments (e.g., manufacturing). Organic structures feature cross-functional teams, decentralized authority, and low formalization; they thrive in dynamic, uncertain environments (e.g., tech startups). The key contingency factor is environmental uncertainty.

questions.question: According to Herzberg's two-factor theory, why does simply increasing an employee's salary not guarantee increased job satisfaction?
questions.answer: Herzberg classified salary as a hygiene factor, not a motivator. Hygiene factors (salary, working conditions, job security) only prevent dissatisfaction when adequate but do not create satisfaction. True satisfaction comes from motivators such as achievement, recognition, meaningful work, and growth opportunities. Therefore, raising salary removes a source of complaint but does not inspire engagement or motivation.

rubric.key: Correct classification of salary as hygiene factor
Distinction between hygiene and motivator effects

major_assignments.title: Management Analysis Case Study
major_assignments.description: Select a real company that has undergone a significant organizational change in the past five years. Analyze the management decisions that drove the change using course frameworks including SWOT, leadership theories, and organizational design principles.
objectives: Apply management theory to a real-world business situation
Demonstrate critical analysis of management decisions
Evaluate the effectiveness of organizational change strategies
Present findings in a professional written format
deliverables: Written case analysis (8-10 pages, APA format)
SWOT analysis diagram with narrative explanation
Leadership style assessment of key decision makers

criteria: Critical evaluation rather than mere description
Use of primary sources (annual reports, press releases)
Comprehensive application of management frameworks to real case
Strong evidence-based analysis with quality sources

major_assignments.title: Organizational Design Proposal
major_assignments.description: Design an organizational structure for a hypothetical company entering a new market. Justify your design choices using organizational theory, addressing departmentalization, span of control, centralization, and reporting relationships.
objectives: Apply organizational design principles to a practical scenario
Justify structural decisions with theoretical support
Consider the impact of structure on strategy execution

criteria: Appropriate span of control for each level
Realistic for stated company size and industry
Response to questions demonstrates deep understanding
Strong theoretical justification for all decisions

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of management theory knowledge and application

class_participation.description: Active engagement in case discussions and group exercises

major_assignments.description: Case analysis and organizational design projects

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment covering management foundations, planning, and organizing

final_examination.description: Cumulative assessment of all course material with emphasis on integration

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. More than 3 unexcused absences will result in a 5% grade reduction per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Extensions must be requested at least 48 hours before the deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Case analyses must be individual work. Group exercises in class are collaborative. All sources must be properly cited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism, fabrication of data, or unauthorized collaboration will result in a zero on the assignment and possible course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop required for in-class exercises. Access to university library databases for research assignments.
